macro_segments.title: Rava's Teaching on Maaser Behemah: All Animals in the Pen and Combination of Leftovers

micro_segments.title: Rava's First Principle: Don't Select the Worst Ten, Put All Animals in the Pen
Rava's Second Principle: Leftover Animals Combine with Next Year's Animals, Not Exempt Until Rosh Hashanah
Braita Supporting Rava: Parallel Teaching on Fifteen Sheep and Proper Procedure
Rashi's Interpretation: Leftovers Cannot Be Used or Sold Until Combined Due to Goren Status

macro_segments.title: The Challenge of Nineteen Sheep: Reconciling Sequential Counting with Leftover Obligations

micro_segments.title: Braita on Nineteen Sheep: Cannot Pick Ten and Ignore Nine; All Must Enter Pen
The Apparent Contradiction: Braita Says Remainder Are Exempt, Earlier Mishnah Says They Combine
Rav Huna bar Sechorah's Resolution: Two-Door Scenario Where Nine Exit Each Door, Allowing Ninth Exemption via Minyan Harauy
Gemara's Alternative Explanation: Two-by-Two Counting Could Also Exempt via Minyan Harauy
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak's Praise: Rav Huna Defended Rava Using Rava's Own Logic

macro_segments.title: The Reality of the Tenth vs. Human Designation: Can Counting Override Objective Status?

micro_segments.title: Core Question: Does Physical Reality Define the Tenth Automatically, or Does Designation Matter?

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Miscounting Nine, Ten, and Eleven: All Three Become Holy But With Different Status

micro_segments.title: Mishnah Case: Called Ninth Ten, Tenth Nine, Eleventh Ten—All Three Become Holy
Status of Ninth: Becomes Holy from Being Called Ten, But Cannot Be Brought as Korban Until Blemished
Status of Tenth: Becomes Holy Both Objectively and from Designation; Brought as Maaser
Status of Eleventh: Becomes Holy from Being Called Ten; Brought as Shelamim
Debate Between Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Meir: Is Eleventh a Temurah or Direct Kedushah?
Principle: Eleventh Only Becomes Holy If You Deny Tenth Its Status (Uprooting the Name)

macro_segments.title: The Reality of the Tenth vs. Human Designation: Can Counting Override Objective Status? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava's Position: The Tenth is Automatically Kadosh Regardless of Miscounting (e.g., Calling Ten One)

macro_segments.title: Two-by-Two and Pair Counting: Does Designation of Grouping Override One-by-One Reality?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan: Counting by Pairs or Hundreds—Tenth of the Grouping Becomes Maaser
Dispute: Does Custom Grouping Matter, or Only One-by-One Sequential Counting?
Gemara's Position: Intentional Regrouping Is Ignored; Only One-by-One Counting Matters

macro_segments.title: The Reality of the Tenth vs. Human Designation: Can Counting Override Objective Status? (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Counting Backward Test: If Counted Backward from Ten to One, Is the First Called One Actually the Tenth?

macro_segments.title: When Two Animals Exit Simultaneously: Does Minyan Harauy Apply or Do Both Become Mixed?

micro_segments.title: Ninth and Tenth Exit Together: Both Called Nine—Mixed Up as Maaser and Chullin
Ninth and Tenth Exit Together: Both Called Ten—Mixed Up as Maaser and Non-Korban Kedushah
Tenth and Eleventh Exit Together: Both Called Ten—Can Both Become Kodesh Even Without Denying Tenth Status?
Principle: Simultaneous Exit Means No Competition; Both Can Be Holy Without Denying Tenth
Clarification: Braita Refers to Sequential Cases; Rule About Denying Status Applies Broadly

macro_segments.title: What Counts as Denying the Tenth Its Status: Active Mislabeling vs. Mere Silence

micro_segments.title: Question: If Tenth Exits and You Say Nothing, Does Calling Eleventh Ten Still Make Eleventh Holy?
Answer: Must Actively Mislabel (Not Just Remain Silent) to Deny Tenth Its Status

macro_segments.title: Rabbi's Position on Calling Eleventh as Ten: Is That Sufficient Mislabeling?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi's View: Calling Eleventh Ten Is Not Sufficient Mislabeling of Tenth (Too Early/Meaningless)
Rava's Clarification: Rabbi's Position Limited to Cases With Many Animals (Achad Asar Ambiguous)
Implication: If Few Animals, Calling Ten Eleven Would Be Proper Mislabeling

macro_segments.title: Practical Consequences: Mixed Maaser and Shelamim—What to Do With Blood and Priestly Portions

micro_segments.title: Problem: Different Ritual Requirements—Maaser One Blood Throw vs. Shelamim Two; Priestly Portions Differ
Case: Tenth and Eleventh Both Called Ten, Creating Mixed Maaser and Shelamim Status
Solution Approach One (Rabbanan): Let Both Graze Until Blemished, Eat as Non-Sacred Meat
Reason for Grazing: Cannot Risk Priestly Portions Not Being Eaten Due to Glut (Beis Hapesul)
Solution Approach Two (Rabbi Shimon): Bring Both as Korbanos Despite Risk of Portions Not Eaten
